content.text: In the general discussion channel, brief greetings were exchanged between community members Manatizer and Jake the muss boii. Separately, Uthman from ApexLabs reached out to jin via direct message, requesting that jin check a screenshot that had been sent privately.

In the coders channel, Odilitime noted that blockchains make good relays. BinaryCookies expressed frustration with their coding progress, mentioning difficulty with movement functionality in their project, though they had made some progress getting chat working in a bubble interface. Additionally, a message in the channel was flagged as a scam by community member Die Antwoord in response to another user.

In the general discussion channel, community members exchanged casual greetings throughout the day, with several users saying hi and hello across different time zones. One user shared a Mother's Day message wishing all mothers a happy day. A developer named Ginga posted a professional introduction, describing themselves as someone who builds AI and full-stack systems focused on practical, production-ready software. They emphasized clean, maintainable, and secure systems over hype, and invited others to reach out for collaboration.

In the coders channel, a user asked about the cost of running an Eliza agent as a Twitter bot on a daily basis. Odilitime responded that it used to cost around 100 dollars per month but has since dropped to approximately 10 dollars per month, though the actual cost depends on configuration choices such as the number of replies and other usage factors. Separately, one user flagged a message as a scam, and another user questioned whether something had been compromised, suggesting some security awareness activity in the channel.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Developer Activity - May 11, 2026

content.text: In the general discussion channel, several AI and full-stack engineers introduced themselves and their services, highlighting expertise in areas such as autonomous agents, LLMs, RAG pipelines, voice AI, computer vision, and multimodal systems. Frameworks mentioned included LangChain, AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph, and various orchestration tools. One community member raised a question about whether the team is still actively supporting the ElizaOS token, noting that Shaw had removed ElizaOS from his X bio, though no official response was provided in the thread. Brief market commentary suggested optimism, with one user noting conditions were favorable and that a catalyst was all that was needed.

In the coders channel, a developer introduced a multi-agent orchestrator built in Python using the claude-agent-sdk, featuring an A2A-protocol read-only research agent with tools including Tavily, DuckDuckGo, fetch, and arXiv via MCP. The developer requested permission to test the agent in a sandbox channel on the server, specifying it would be read-only, send no DMs, and only respond to direct mentions. They also mentioned having emailed inquiries@elizalabs.ai and asked about OAuth invite or whitelist access. Odilitime, a core moderator and developer, responded directing the user to DM them regarding the OAuth process.

In the general discussion channel, a developer named Ginga introduced themselves as a builder of AI and full-stack systems, emphasizing practical software delivery over hype, with a focus on clean, maintainable, and secure production-ready systems. The introduction received a positive reaction from the community. Separately, a user named DON DADA greeted the channel and asked if anyone needed chatters.

In the coders channel, a developer named sailorpepe.eth reported that the v2 plugin registry infrastructure appears to be unavailable, with both the elizaos-plugins/registry repo and plugins.elizacloud.ai returning 404 errors. They had a third-party plugin ready for submission and asked whether to hold their pull request until the repo goes public or if a staging area exists. Core developer Odilitime acknowledged the issue and suggested the team may revert to accepting pull requests directly to the elizaOS/eliza repository. Additionally, community members flagged suspicious activity in the Discord, with one user suggesting an admin account may have been compromised. Odilitime clarified that the project has no admins and attributed the suspicious activity to scammers.
No activity was recorded for the date 2026-05-12. There are no events, updates, or notable occurrences to report for this period.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion: V3 Development, Eliza-1 Model Series, and GODL Integration

content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members exchanged greetings and raised questions about v3 development progress. One member asked for clarification on whether current v3 work is primarily focused on infrastructure stabilization or if the full autonomous agent workflow stack is close to being publicly exposed. They also inquired about the integration status of the new identity and AgentID system in the upcoming architecture. No direct response to these technical questions was recorded in the channel.

A community member shared details about the latest Eliza-1 model series, which is based exclusively on Qwen3.5. The lineup includes models ranging from 0.6B, which is the smallest and capable of running offline on any phone, up to a 27B model with a 1 million token context window. A new 0.8B model is currently being trained on H200 hardware. The series is designed to cover a wide range of hardware targets from mobile phones to servers and workstations.
A warning video was shared in the discussion channel in response to another user, though no additional context about its content was provided in the text of the conversation.

The results of an auto.fun AI Sprint competition were shared, announcing the winning teams after weeks of building, community voting, and live demos. First place went to Build4, second place to Clawdyland, with a third place also mentioned.

In the coders channel, a community member working on business development for GODL, a gamified on-chain mining protocol on Solana, reached out to explore potential integration with elizaOS. GODL exposes its system through SDKs, websocket streams, automation hooks, and a public skill.md file designed for agent and tool integrations. The member highlighted potential use cases including autonomous mining and staking strategies, agent-managed wallets, competing mining behaviors, and public leaderboards. Odilitime responded positively, noting that the skill.md file could be used as-is, and agreed to continue the conversation via direct message to avoid bot interference and share further documentation.
No activity was recorded for the date 2026-05-13.

In the general discussion channel, Shaw mentioned that the current setup uses Qwen with some optimizations, and that a fine-tuning pipeline is being developed to improve model performance for calling actions and planning. The channel also saw several users posting self-promotional messages, including one user advertising AI and full-stack development services, and another posting a spam investment solicitation that was warned by a moderator (Odilitime) with a threat of a ban if repeated. A job posting was shared by Sindy seeking testers at 35 per hour, moderators at 450 per week, and a Solidity developer at negotiable pay for an onchain game team, described as remote and flexible.

In the coders channel, ReaWorks shared a project called the Pay or Burn escrow protocol deployed on Base Mainnet, designed specifically for autonomous AI agents. The protocol uses a game-theoretic mechanism where if two agents cannot agree on a task outcome, the escrowed funds are burned, eliminating incentives for malicious behavior. Key architectural choices include 100 percent pull-payment to avoid issues with reverting receiver contracts, no user interface with only a raw smart contract and SDKs in Python and C++, and a burn logic that destroys expected value for bad actors. The project is available on GitHub. Separately, Xavier raised a question about excessive Neynar credit consumption, noting his agent was using around 300 credits per hour on Farcaster notifications instead of the expected 4, and Odilitime suggested that using webhooks could reduce consumption. The coders channel also received the same spam investment message and a self-promotional post from an AI and ML engineer seeking work on LLM systems and agent workflows.
No activity was recorded for the date of 2026-05-14. There is no data or events to report for this period.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Developer Activity - May 15, 2026

content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members exchanged greetings throughout the day. One member, IVgamer, noted excitement about a new partnership announcement, describing it as significant for the project's recognition and growth. A question was raised about an update related to Eliza OZ, to which enigma289 confirmed they were actively working on it. Odilitime responded with a question mark to IVgamer's partnership comment, suggesting some uncertainty or curiosity about the details. Overall the channel saw light social interaction with no major technical discussions.

In the coders channel, a developer named ReaWorks introduced themselves and described a project they are exploring: a simple escrow primitive for AI-agent work built on Base. The design involves a buyer locking funds, an agent or seller completing work, and the buyer releasing payment, with dispute and timeout paths kept minimal to ensure the incentive structure remains auditable. ReaWorks expressed interest in understanding how agent frameworks like elizaOS should interface with payment and settlement flows without embedding excessive trust logic into the agent runtime itself. This reflects a broader interest in integrating financial primitives with AI agent systems in a transparent and secure way.
No activity was recorded for the date 2026-05-15. There is no data available to summarize for this period.
